questions.question: What is the first goal of a messaging drill answer?
options: Choose the primitive by clarifying ownership, replay, and state needs before naming products.
List as many broker technologies as possible.
Skip clarification so more time is left for diagrams.
Assume all asynchronous problems are stream problems.
questions.explanation: The drill rewards clear reasoning about the problem, not product recall.

questions.question: Why should the drill force an explicit duplicate or loss policy?
options: Because delivery guarantees are only meaningful when the system states what failure is acceptable on the critical path.
Because duplicates never happen in managed brokers.
Because loss and duplication are only observability concerns.
Because replay removes all semantic decisions.
questions.explanation: A strong answer must say what failure semantics matter and why.

questions.question: Which answer sounds strongest?
options: I will start with Kafka, then add exactly-once, then mention DLQs if there is time.
I will clarify whether this is task execution, event distribution, or orchestration, then define ordering, delivery, replay, and overload behavior for the critical path.
I will avoid backlog discussion because queues already absorb bursts.
I will treat every consumer as identical to keep the design simple.
questions.explanation: That answer structures the design around the real decision points.

questions.question: What is the hidden risk of adding DLQ, replay, workflow, and streams all at once without a clear reason?
options: The design becomes pattern soup with unclear ownership and unnecessary operational complexity.
The broker automatically rejects the architecture.
The system loses the ability to commit offsets.
The product can no longer support retries.
questions.explanation: A strong drill answer stays selective and intentional.

questions.question: Which metric pair best shows both steady-state health and hidden overload risk?
options: Consumer lag or backlog age together with user-visible completion delay.
Only schema registry object count.
Average markdown heading depth.
The number of package imports in workers.
questions.explanation: Those metrics connect internal pipeline health to real product impact.

questions.question: What trade-off often appears when tightening delivery guarantees late in the drill?
options: You usually pay with more state, more latency, and more coordination work around retries or dedupe.
You automatically simplify the relay and replay paths.
You remove the need for event IDs and observability.
You make hot partitions disappear.
questions.explanation: Stronger guarantees nearly always increase system and operational cost.

questions.question: Why is hot-key skew important even in a high-level drill?
options: Because one partition or ordering key can become the real throughput limit even when the rest of the fleet looks healthy.
Because hot keys only matter in databases, not streams.
Because skew eliminates the need for consumer groups.
Because replay traffic always balances partitions automatically.
questions.explanation: Skew can defeat naive scaling assumptions in event systems.

questions.question: What should usually come last in a strong messaging drill answer?
options: A concise wrap-up on observability, failure handling, and the main trade-offs of the chosen async model.
A long list of unrelated storage products.
An apology for not covering every messaging pattern.
A restatement that all systems eventually use the same broker.
questions.explanation: Senior answers close by making trade-offs and operational behavior explicit.
